symbols: bound arms raised overhead — submission to suffering and union with Christ's Passion
exposed torso — vulnerability and stripping of earthly dignity, echoing Christ stripped before crucifixion
rope binding — captivity and the bonds of martyrdom willingly accepted
rock/stone beneath — the firmness of faith upon which the martyr stands
description: A muscular, mostly nude elderly man hangs with his arms bound above his head to a vertical post or beam. His body twists under the strain, head bowed forward. A smaller, darker-clad figure stands beside him, apparently an executioner or torturer tending to the bound man's legs or lower body. The scene is stark, set against a plain background with rough stones at the base.
theological_description: The image of a saint bound and suspended in torment directly evokes the Passion of Christ, particularly the scourging at the pillar (John 19:1). Patristic theology, especially in Ignatius of Antioch and the early martyrologies, understood the martyr's body as a living epistle of Christ's suffering — the more the flesh is torn, the more the inner glory of grace is revealed. The strappado posture, arms wrenched upward, mirrors the cruciform shape, making the martyr a living icon of Calvary. Augustine taught that the martyrs do not suffer in their own strength but in Christ who suffers in them (Totus Christus). The exposed, aged body recalls Paul's boast in bearing the marks of Jesus (Galatians 6:17). The executioner, small beside the saint, illustrates the paradox of martyrdom: the one who appears powerless holds true spiritual authority, while the torturer is diminished. This resonates with 2 Corinthians 12:9 — power made perfect in weakness.

scripture_refs.reason: The bound, scourged posture directly recalls Christ's scourging before Pilate

Co-suffering with Christ as condition for co-glorification

Sharing in Christ's sufferings, becoming like him in death
